content:
# 1 Peter Chapter 5 — Disagreements Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Agents:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Date:** 2026-03-06

---

## Verse 1 — Opening address and self-identification

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "[The] elders among you I exhort, the fellow-elder and witness of the sufferings of the Christos, the [one] also [being] a sharer of the glory about to be revealed" |
| LOGOS | "The elders among you I exhort, I who am a fellow elder and a witness of the sufferings of Christ, and also a partaker of the glory that is about to be revealed" |
| PNEUMA | "So I appeal to the elders among you, as a fellow elder myself and a witness of Christ's sufferings, and as one who will share in the glory that is about to be revealed" |

**Disagreement:** (1) EMET retains "Christos" untranslated; LOGOS and PNEUMA use "Christ." (2) PNEUMA adds "So" and uses "appeal" instead of "exhort" (parakaleo). (3) EMET has "sharer," LOGOS has "partaker," PNEUMA has "one who will share in" -- differing translations of ΚΟΙΝΩΝΟΣ. (4) PNEUMA restructures the sentence significantly from the Greek word order.
**SOPHIA's ruling:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering as closest to the Greek structure while remaining fluent. "Christ" preferred over "Christos" for consistency with English convention. "Partaker" chosen over "sharer" for its slightly more formal register befitting the context. PNEUMA's "appeal" rejected in favor of "exhort" (standard translation of parakaleo in epistolary contexts).

---

## Verse 2 — Shepherding instruction

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "Shepherd the flock of the God among you, overseeing not by compulsion but willingly according to God, not for shameful gain but eagerly" |
| LOGOS | "Shepherd the flock of God that is among you, exercising oversight not under compulsion but willingly, according to God -- not for shameful gain but eagerly" |
| PNEUMA | "Be shepherds of God's flock that is in your care. Watch over them -- not because you have to, but because you want to, as God desires. Not for shameful profit, but with an eager heart." |

**Disagreement:** (1) "Overseeing" vs. "exercising oversight" vs. "Watch over them" for ΕΠΙΣΚΟΠΟΥΝΤΕΣ. (2) PNEUMA breaks into multiple sentences and adds interpretive expansions ("because you have to," "because you want to," "with an eager heart"). (3) "Shameful gain" vs. "shameful profit."
**SOPHIA's ruling:** "Exercising oversight" (LOGOS) retains the episkopos-related vocabulary, which is textually significant. PNEUMA's paraphrase loses this important connection. "Shameful gain" preferred over "profit" as more literal to ΑΙΣΧΡΟΚΕΡΔΩΣ.

---

## Verse 3 — Not lording over

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "not as lording over the allotted [ones], but becoming examples of the flock" |
| LOGOS | "not as lording it over those allotted to your charge, but becoming examples to the flock" |
| PNEUMA | "Don't lord it over those entrusted to you. Instead, be examples that the flock can follow." |

**Disagreement:** (1) "Allotted ones" vs. "those allotted to your charge" vs. "those entrusted to you" for ΤΩΝ ΚΛΗΡΩΝ. (2) PNEUMA adds "that the flock can follow" (not in Greek). (3) "Examples of the flock" vs. "examples to the flock."
**SOPHIA's ruling:** LOGOS's "those allotted to your charge" best conveys the ΚΛΗΡΩΝ concept. "Examples to the flock" (LOGOS) preferred over "of the flock" (EMET) as a better rendering of the dative ΤΥΠΟΙ ... ΤΟΥ ΠΟΙΜΝΙΟΥ. PNEUMA's additions rejected as not present in the source text.

---

## Verse 5 — Humility instruction and Proverbs citation

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | 'And all [of you], toward one another, clothe yourselves with humility, because "The God opposes [the] proud, but gives grace to [the] humble."' |
| LOGOS | "And all of you, clothe yourselves with humility toward one another, for God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble." |
| PNEUMA | "And all of you, clothe yourselves with humility toward one another, because God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble." |

**Disagreement:** (1) EMET uses quotation marks around the Proverbs 3:34 citation; LOGOS and PNEUMA integrate it. (2) "Because" vs. "for" as connector. (3) EMET retains "The God" with articles.
**SOPHIA's ruling:** Removed quotation marks (following LOGOS/PNEUMA) since the Greek does not mark the quotation distinctively. Used "for" (LOGOS) as the connector. Dropped the articles before "God" for natural English.

---

## Verse 6 — Humbling and exaltation

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "Be humbled therefore under the mighty hand of the God, that he may exalt you in [the] time" |
| LOGOS | "Humble yourselves, therefore, under the mighty hand of God, so that he may exalt you in due time" |
| PNEUMA | "So humble yourselves under the mighty hand of God, and in due time he will lift you up." |

**Disagreement:** (1) "Be humbled" (passive, EMET) vs. "Humble yourselves" (middle/reflexive, LOGOS/PNEUMA). The Greek ΤΑΠΕΙΝΩΘΗΤΕ is aorist passive imperative, but with middle sense. (2) "In [the] time" (EMET, literal of ΕΝ ΚΑΙΡΩ) vs. "in due time" (LOGOS/PNEUMA, interpretive). (3) "Exalt" vs. "lift you up" (PNEUMA). (4) PNEUMA changes subjunctive "that he may" to indicative "he will."
**SOPHIA's ruling:** "Humble yourselves" (middle sense) is the standard and correct reading. "In due time" adopted as an appropriate rendering of ΕΝ ΚΑΙΡΩ, which implies an appointed time. "Exalt" preferred over PNEUMA's "lift you up" for register. Subjunctive "so that he may" retained over PNEUMA's indicative.

---

## Verse 7 — Casting anxiety

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "having cast all the anxiety of you upon him, because it matters to him concerning you" |
| LOGOS | "casting all your anxiety upon him, because he cares for you" |
| PNEUMA | "Turn all your worries over to him, because he cares about you deeply." |

**Disagreement:** (1) "Having cast" (aorist participle, EMET) vs. "casting" (LOGOS) vs. "Turn over" (PNEUMA, imperatival paraphrase). (2) "Anxiety" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "worries" (PNEUMA) for ΜΕΡΙΜΝΑΝ. (3) "It matters to him concerning you" (EMET) vs. "he cares for you" (LOGOS) vs. "he cares about you deeply" (PNEUMA, with added "deeply").
**SOPHIA's ruling:** "Casting" (LOGOS) balances the participial form without the over-literal "having cast." "Anxiety" retained as a closer match to ΜΕΡΙΜΝΑΝ. "He cares for you" (LOGOS) is concise and accurate; PNEUMA's "deeply" is interpretive addition.

---

## Verse 8 — The devil as lion

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "The adversary of you, [the] devil, as a roaring lion walks about, seeking whom to swallow up" |
| LOGOS | "Your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour" |
| PNEUMA | "Your enemy the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, looking for someone to devour." |

**Disagreement:** (1) "Adversary" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "enemy" (PNEUMA) for ΑΝΤΙΔΙΚΟΣ. (2) "Walks about" (EMET) vs. "prowls around" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for ΠΕΡΙΠΑΤΕΙ. (3) "Swallow up" (EMET) vs. "devour" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for ΚΑΤΑΠΙΕΙΝ. (4) "Seeking whom" (EMET) vs. "seeking someone" (LOGOS) vs. "looking for someone" (PNEUMA).
**SOPHIA's ruling:** "Adversary" retained (EMET/LOGOS) as it preserves the legal-forensic sense of ΑΝΤΙΔΙΚΟΣ. "Prowls" preferred over "walks about" for contextual vividness. "Devour" preferred over "swallow up" as more natural English for predatory consumption.

---

## Verse 9 — Resisting in faith

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "whom resist, firm in the faith, knowing the same [kinds] of sufferings are being accomplished upon the brotherhood of you in [the] world" |
| LOGOS | "Resist him, firm in the faith, knowing that the same sufferings are being accomplished by your brotherhood throughout the world" |
| PNEUMA | "Stand firm against him, solid in your faith, knowing that your fellow believers throughout the world are enduring the same kinds of suffering." |

**Disagreement:** (1) "Whom resist" (EMET, following Greek word order) vs. "Resist him" (LOGOS) vs. "Stand firm against him" (PNEUMA). (2) "Brotherhood" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "fellow believers" (PNEUMA) for ΑΔΕΛΦΟΤΗΤΙ. (3) "Are being accomplished" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "are enduring" (PNEUMA) for ΕΠΙΤΕΛΕΙΣΘΑΙ. (4) "Upon" (EMET) vs. "by" (LOGOS) -- different prepositional rendering. (5) "Same [kinds] of sufferings" (EMET) vs. "same sufferings" (LOGOS) vs. "same kinds of suffering" (PNEUMA).
**SOPHIA's ruling:** "Resist him" (LOGOS) adopted for clarity. "Brotherhood" retained over "fellow believers" to preserve the specific Greek term. "Are being accomplished" kept as it reflects the passive voice suggesting divine orchestration. "Same kinds of sufferings" adopted as a blend of EMET and PNEUMA, reflecting the partitive genitive.

---

## Verse 10 — Four restoration verbs

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "he himself will restore, will establish, will strengthen, will ground [you]" |
| LOGOS | "will himself restore, confirm, strengthen, and establish you" |
| PNEUMA | "will himself restore you, strengthen you, establish you, and set you on a firm foundation" |

**Disagreement:** (1) Verb order: EMET follows the Greek (restore, establish, strengthen, ground); LOGOS reorders (restore, confirm, strengthen, establish); PNEUMA reorders (restore, strengthen, establish, foundation). (2) ΣΤΗΡΙΞΕΙ: "establish" (EMET) vs. "confirm" (LOGOS) vs. "establish" (PNEUMA). (3) ΘΕΜΕΛΙΩΣΕΙ: "ground" (EMET) vs. "establish" (LOGOS) vs. "set you on a firm foundation" (PNEUMA). (4) LOGOS uses "confirm" for ΣΤΗΡΙΞΕΙ and "establish" for ΘΕΜΕΛΙΩΣΕΙ, conflating the distinction.
**SOPHIA's ruling:** Followed the Greek verb order per Sinaiticus: ΚΑΤΑΡΤΙΣΕΙ (restore), ΣΤΗΡΙΞΕΙ (establish), ΣΘΕΝΩΣΕΙ (strengthen), ΘΕΜΕΛΙΩΣΕΙ (ground). EMET's verb choices are most faithful to the Greek distinctions. "Ground" for ΘΕΜΕΛΙΩΣΕΙ preserves the architectural/foundation metaphor distinct from "establish." LOGOS's reordering and PNEUMA's paraphrase rejected.

---

## Verse 11 — Doxology length

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "To him [be] the dominion into the ages. Amen." |
| LOGOS | "To him be the dominion unto the ages of the ages. Amen." |
| PNEUMA | "To him belongs all power forever and ever. Amen." |

**Disagreement:** (1) EMET follows Sinaiticus shorter reading (ΕΙΣ ΤΟΥΣ ΑΙΩΝΑΣ); LOGOS adds "of the ages" (ΤΩΝ ΑΙΩΝΩΝ), reflecting a later manuscript tradition. (2) PNEUMA paraphrases entirely ("all power forever and ever"). (3) "Dominion" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "all power" (PNEUMA) for ΤΟ ΚΡΑΤΟΣ.
**SOPHIA's ruling:** Sinaiticus clearly reads the shorter form. LOGOS's "of the ages" represents a later textual addition and is rejected. PNEUMA's "all power forever and ever" is too free. SOPHIA follows EMET's text-critical fidelity but uses LOGOS's "unto" for slightly more formal register.

---

## Verse 12 — Letter through Silvanus

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "Through Silvanos, the faithful brother as I reckon, through few [words] I wrote to you, exhorting and testifying this to be [the] true grace of the God, into which stand." |
| LOGOS | "Through Silvanus, the faithful brother -- as I regard him -- I have written to you briefly, exhorting and testifying that this is the true grace of God. Stand firm in it." |
| PNEUMA | "I have written you this brief letter with the help of Silvanus, whom I regard as a faithful brother. My purpose has been to encourage you and to testify that this is the true grace of God. Stand firm in it." |

**Disagreement:** (1) "Silvanos" (EMET) vs. "Silvanus" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). (2) Sentence structure: EMET follows Greek closely; LOGOS restructures moderately; PNEUMA restructures extensively with added interpretation ("My purpose has been"). (3) "Into which stand" (EMET, literal) vs. "Stand firm in it" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). (4) "I wrote" (aorist, EMET) vs. "I have written" (LOGOS/PNEUMA).
**SOPHIA's ruling:** "Silvanus" adopted as the standard English form. LOGOS's moderate restructuring provides clarity without PNEUMA's interpretive additions. "Stand firm in it" adopted as a clear rendering of ΕΙΣ ΗΝ ΣΤΗΤΕ. "I have written" preferred as standard English epistolary past.

---

## Verse 13 — Babylon and the co-chosen

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "She in Babylon, [the] co-chosen [one], greets you, and Markos the son of me." |
| LOGOS | "She who is in Babylon, chosen together with you, greets you, and so does Mark, my son." |
| PNEUMA | "The church in Babylon, chosen together with you, sends you greetings. So does my son Mark." |

**Disagreement:** (1) PNEUMA makes explicit "the church" where the Greek has only the feminine article Η (understood as referring to ΕΚΚΛΗΣΙΑ). EMET and LOGOS preserve the formal ambiguity. (2) "Markos" (EMET) vs. "Mark" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). (3) "Greets" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "sends you greetings" (PNEUMA). (4) "The son of me" (EMET) vs. "my son" (LOGOS/PNEUMA).
**SOPHIA's ruling:** "She who is in Babylon" (LOGOS) preserves the deliberate obliqueness of the Greek without making the interpretive leap to "the church." "Mark" adopted as standard English form. "My son" preferred over "the son of me" for natural English.

---

## General Disagreements

### Section headings
PNEUMA inserts section headings ("Counsel for Elders and the Whole Community," "Trust God with Your Worries," "Final Greetings"). EMET and LOGOS do not.
**SOPHIA's ruling:** No section headings in the consensus text. They are not part of the manuscript.

### Sentence-ending punctuation
PNEUMA consistently adds periods at the end of every verse. EMET and LOGOS are inconsistent.
**SOPHIA's ruling:** Consistent punctuation applied throughout, following standard English conventions.

### Articles before "God"
EMET frequently retains the Greek article before "God" ("the God"). LOGOS and PNEUMA drop it per English convention.
**SOPHIA's ruling:** Articles before "God" dropped in English, as the Greek article before ΘΕΟΣ is grammatical rather than theological, and English treats "God" as a proper noun without articles.

---

**Summary:** 13 verse-level disagreements logged across 14 verses. The primary axis of disagreement remains PNEUMA's tendency to paraphrase, add interpretive content, and restructure sentences, versus EMET's extreme literalism that sometimes produces awkward English. LOGOS consistently occupies the middle ground. SOPHIA's consensus leaned most heavily on LOGOS (approximately 60% of renderings), supplemented by EMET's precision on text-critical matters (verse 10 verb order, verse 11 doxology length) and occasional vocabulary choices. PNEUMA's renderings were rarely adopted in full but contributed to readability considerations.
